mnemonics.title: Trend Forecasting Methods
mnemonics.content: MELDS: Moving average, Exponential smoothing, Linear regression, Delphi method, Seasonal decomposition - a comprehensive forecasting toolkit for capacity planners.

mnemonics.title: Forecasting Accuracy Metrics
mnemonics.content: MAPE: Mean Absolute Percentage Error - measures prediction accuracy as average percentage deviation from actual values.

mnemonics.title: Capacity Planning Buffer
mnemonics.content: HEADROOM: Hold Extra At Designed Resource Or More - remember to add 15-30% buffer above predicted demand for unexpected spikes.

acronyms.usage: Measures forecasting accuracy by calculating average percentage difference between predicted and actual values
Measures forecasting accuracy by averaging squared differences between predicted and actual values
Forecasting technique that averages the last n data points to smooth short-term fluctuations
Forecasting technique that assigns different weights to different time periods, usually favoring recent data
Forecasting method giving exponentially decreasing weight to older observations
Advanced time series forecasting model combining autoregression, differencing, and moving average

visualTips.visualization: Draw a straight diagonal line for linear growth, and a J-shaped curve for exponential growth - helps visualize capacity requirements over time
Picture a repeating wave pattern (sine wave) over time - peaks and valleys at regular intervals representing cyclical demand
Upward slope = increasing demand (scale up), flat = stable demand, downward slope = decreasing demand (optimize resources)
Think of a glass filled to 70% leaving 30% empty space - the empty space represents headroom for unexpected demand spikes

analogies.analogy: Like taking a photograph with long exposure - individual movements blur together to show the overall path you're traveling
Like a fading memory - you remember recent events clearly but older events become vague, influencing your predictions accordingly
Like the foundation of a building - the minimum level that must always exist to support everything above it
Like extra seats in a theater - having buffer capacity prevents sold-out situations when demand unexpectedly increases
Like tide patterns - just as tides rise and fall predictably due to moon phases, IT demand rises and falls due to business cycles
Like the general direction of a hiker's path - ignoring ups and downs to see if they're generally climbing, descending, or staying level
